main_claims.claim: The DOV controversy can be reformulated as the minimum number of apparatuses or standards needed for distinct laboratories to compare observable values.
main_claims.notes: This is a coherent operational framing, but it is a definition of the problem rather than a consequence forced by DOV. It narrows 'fundamental constants' to dimensional standards and does not address dimensionless constants.

main_claims.claim: Galilei spacetime requires bona fide clocks and rulers, whereas Minkowski/relativistic spacetime requires bona fide clocks only.
main_claims.notes: The clock/ruler contrast is plausible at the level of metric structure. However, the claim that clocks alone define Minkowski spacetime relies on causal structure plus proper-time standards and idealized lightlike or limiting procedures, and needs sharper hypotheses for general relativistic spacetimes.

main_claims.claim: In Galilei spacetime, all observables can be expressed using only space and time units by absorbing mass into G.
main_claims.notes: The dimensional conversion is algebraically clear once Newtonian gravity with universal G and equivalence of inertial and gravitational mass is assumed. It is not shown for arbitrary theories over Galilei spacetime, nor for sectors where gravitational calibration is unavailable or not part of the theory.
main_claims.paper_location: Section 'Two units to rule us all in Galilei spacetime', Eqs. (13)-(15)

main_claims.claim: In relativistic spacetimes, all observables can be expressed using only one time unit; hence the number of fundamental constants is one.
main_claims.notes: The length-from-clocks protocol is a useful local construction in Minkowski spacetime. The extension from spacetime intervals to all observables again uses additional assumptions such as G, k_e, k_B and c as universal conversion factors and does not fully characterize the class of observables.
main_claims.paper_location: Sections 'Time to rule us all in relativistic spacetimes' and 'One fundamental constant', especially Eqs. (18)-(22)

main_claims.claim: Equation (18) gives a speed-independent clock-only determination of a rod's proper length in Minkowski spacetime.
main_claims.notes: Within the idealized Minkowski setup with inertial clocks and a rod at rest in an inertial congruence, the derivation in Appendix B follows from the line element. Its general-relativistic use is only local.
main_claims.paper_location: Section 'Time to rule us all in relativistic spacetimes' and Appendix B

missing_assumptions.assumption: A fixed class of physical theories and observables is being considered, not literally all theories constructible on Galilei or relativistic spacetime.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Introduction and repeated claims in Sections 'Two units to rule us all...' and 'Time to rule us all...'
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Without this scope restriction, the proof by dimensional conversion is too broad; one can add fields or sectors whose dimensional normalization is not fixed by the stated spacetime apparatuses alone.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Newtonian gravity with universal, constant G and equivalence of inertial and gravitational mass is available when reducing MKS to MS in Galilei spacetime.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Section 'Two units to rule us all in Galilei spacetime'
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: This is the only mechanism given for eliminating the mass standard. It is a dynamical-law assumption, not a consequence of Galilei spacetime.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The constants k_B, k_e, G and c are universal conversion factors for the intended measurement domain and can consistently be set to 1 without losing operational comparability.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Sections 'Recuperating the MKS system from the SI' and 'Time to rule us all in relativistic spacetimes'
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The one-standard conclusion depends on these constants being usable as stable conversion conventions across laboratories.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Ideal bona fide clocks and, where needed, rods/inertial congruences exist to arbitrary required precision or at least within the regime of the claim.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Section 'Galilei and Minkowski spacetimes'; Closing remarks
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The authors acknowledge quantum limits to clocks, but the main argument assumes idealized apparatuses. The domain where the conclusion holds should be explicitly classical/semiclassical.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does the literature already contain the same spacetime-first answer to DOV, especially via Geroch-style operational spacetime constructions or geometrized units?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Novelty relative to specialized philosophy/metrology literature requires a targeted expert literature check beyond the provided bundle.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the Unruh clock protocol in Eq. (18) known and correctly attributed, and are its assumptions sufficient for the stated general-relativistic local extension?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The derivation is locally checkable in Minkowski spacetime, but provenance and curved-spacetime generality need expert verification.

novelty_assessment: The paper's main conceptual contribution is a clean spacetime-first reframing of the DOV dispute and a nice presentation of a clock-only length protocol. The dimensional reductions themselves are close to standard geometrized-units reasoning. Novelty is therefore moderate: the synthesis is useful, but the claim to have definitively resolved the controversy is overstated unless the authors narrow the scope and hypotheses.
score_rationale: Clear and thought-provoking, with several locally sound dimensional and spacetime arguments. I lower technical soundness because the strongest conclusions depend on unstated dynamical and scope assumptions, especially the use of gravity to eliminate mass and the leap from clock-only spacetime measurements to all observables. Worth surfacing as a promising conceptual contribution, not as a certified resolution of the full controversy.
summary: The paper gives a spacetime-first operational answer to the Duff-Okun-Veneziano question: how many dimensional standards are minimally needed to compare all physical observables. It argues that Galilei spacetime requires two standards, clocks and rulers, while relativistic spacetimes require only one, clocks, because lengths and then all observables can be expressed in time units. The exposition is clear and often useful as a dimensional-analysis/metrology argument, but several key reductions depend on physical-law assumptions that are not fully stated and the universal quantifier over all observables/theories is stronger than what is locally proved.

technical_objections.evidence: The paper states that GM is measured via gravitational acceleration and then multiplies every MKS observable by G^{gamma_i}. This only works if every mass dimension can be calibrated through universal gravitation and if inertial and gravitational masses are equivalent. The paper briefly notes the equivalence-principle issue, but the headline claim is phrased for 'all observables of our theories defined in Galilei spacetime.'
technical_objections.objection: The reduction of mass to length and time in Galilei spacetime is not a property of Galilei spacetime alone; it imports Newtonian gravity, a universal constant G, and the equivalence of inertial and gravitational mass.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section 'Two units to rule us all in Galilei spacetime', paragraph beginning 'Maxwell’s statement emphasizes...' and Eqs. (13)-(15)

technical_objections.evidence: The paper models observables as monomials in m, s, kg or in m, s with real powers. It does not specify whether dimensionless coupling constants, particle identity labels, topological quantities, intensive thermodynamic variables, gauge-normalization conventions, or theory-dependent observables fall within the quantifier. The proof depends on the representation of observables by dimensional exponents rather than on a general measurement protocol for all observables.
technical_objections.objection: The universal claim over 'all observables' is underdefined. The argument shows a dimensional re-expression of quantities once a unit system and conversion constants are accepted, not that every physically measurable observable can be operationally compared using only the stated apparatuses.
technical_objections.paper_location: Introduction; Section 'Recuperating the MKS system from the SI'; Eqs. (14)-(15), (19)-(22)

technical_objections.evidence: Eq. (18) supports measuring a rod length in time units. But the subsequent move to all observables assumes that k_B, k_e, G and c are merely conversion factors and that all mass, charge and temperature dimensions can be folded into those constants. This is not entailed by relativistic spacetime structure itself; it uses empirical facts about the physical laws being considered.
technical_objections.objection: The step from clock-only measurement of spacetime length to clock-only expression of all non-spacetime observables in relativistic theories requires extra dynamical assumptions that are not isolated as hypotheses.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section 'Time to rule us all in relativistic spacetimes', transition from Eq. (18) to Eqs. (19)-(20); Section 'One fundamental constant'

technical_objections.evidence: The paper says the conclusions hold in any relativistic spacetime, with small-neighborhood caveats. The explicit proofs use inertial congruences, rods at rest, global Minkowski coordinates, and freely moving clocks along the rod. In curved spacetime, simultaneity hypersurfaces, geodesic convexity, tidal corrections, acceleration of rod endpoints, and clock transport conditions require careful local hypotheses.
technical_objections.objection: The general-relativistic extension is asserted more broadly than derived.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section 'Minkowski spacetime' after Eq. (4); Section 'Time to rule us all in relativistic spacetimes' after Eq. (18); Appendix B final paragraph

technical_objections.evidence: The paper offers a defensible convention tied to standards and dimensional units, but Duff-style objections about the physical significance of only dimensionless variations are not fully refuted. The reply that e^S and hbar^S can be compared with clocks presupposes acceptance of the chosen geometrized dimensional standards rather than showing that these comparisons have invariant physical content independent of convention.
technical_objections.objection: The novelty/resolution claim is stronger than what the argument establishes.
technical_objections.paper_location: Abstract and Introduction, phrases 'set to rest', 'one-answer question', and 'aims to resolve'
